programs.name: Health First Colorado Buy-In (QMB, SLMB, QI)
programs.tagline: If your parent has Medicare and limited income, Colorado may cover their premiums, deductibles, and copays.

localEntities.name: Apply via any of Colorado's 64 county Departments of Human Services.

programs.intro:
If your parent is 65+ with Medicare and living on a fixed income, Health First Colorado's Medicare Buy-In programs can cover their Medicare costs. The Qualified Medicare Beneficiary (QMB) program provides the most help — it pays the $185/month Medicare Part B premium plus all deductibles, copays, and the 20% coinsurance that Medicare doesn't cover. The Specified Low-Income Medicare Beneficiary (SLMB) and Qualifying Individual (QI) programs cover only the Part B premium.

Three income tiers determine which level of help your parent gets: QMB covers people with income up to about $1,275/month for a single person, SLMB covers $1,531-$1,830/month, and QI covers $1,831-$2,056/month. All programs have asset limits of $9,090 for an individual or $13,630 for a couple. Your parent must already have Medicare Part A to qualify.

The QI program has limited federal funding and operates first-come, first-served, so it may close enrollment during the year. Apply through your local county human services office — processing typically takes 45-90 days.
programs.savingsSource: Medicare Part B premiums ($185/month) plus potential deductibles and copays for QMB tier

exemptAssets: Life insurance up to $1,500 face value

applicationGuide.summary: Apply online at Health First Colorado or through your county human services office — processing takes 45-90 days.

steps.title: Gather Medicare and financial documents
steps.description: You'll need your parent's Medicare card, recent bank statements, pay stubs or Social Security award letter, and proof of residency.

steps.title: Complete Health First Colorado application
steps.description: Apply online or visit your county Department of Human Services office. Indicate that your parent has Medicare when applying.

steps.title: Submit required documentation
steps.description: Provide proof of income, assets, Medicare enrollment, and Colorado residency.

steps.title: Wait for eligibility determination
steps.description: County will review application and determine which tier (QMB, SLMB, or QI) your parent qualifies for based on income.

applicationGuide.waitlist: QI program may have waitlist due to federal funding caps
applicationGuide.tip: Apply early in the calendar year for QI program before federal funding runs out.

contentSections.heading: Income Limits by Program Tier (2026 estimates)
contentSections.footnote: Estimated 2026 figures based on federal poverty guidelines

contentSections.heading: Asset Limits: What Counts
excluded: Life insurance policies up to $1,500 face value

tiers.name: QMB (Qualified Medicare Beneficiary)
tiers.description: Lowest income tier with most coverage
tiers.coverage: Medicare Part B premium ($185/month) + all deductibles, copays, and 20% coinsurance

tiers.name: QI (Qualifying Individual)
tiers.description: Highest income tier with limited funding

categories.name: Identity and Residency
items: Birth certificate or proof of age

categories.name: Medicare Information
items: Medicare card showing Part A enrollment

categories.name: Financial Records
items: Pay stubs or Social Security award letter

contentSections.text: QI program funding is limited and operates first-come, first-served. It may close enrollment partway through the year when federal money runs out.
Don't confuse this with Colorado's other Buy-In programs for disabled adults under 65. This program is specifically for people 65+ with Medicare Part A.

faqs.question: Can Mom keep her house if she qualifies?
faqs.answer: Yes. The primary home and land don't count toward the $9,090 asset limit for individuals or $13,630 for couples.

faqs.question: What if her income is just over the QMB limit but under SLMB?
faqs.answer: She would qualify for SLMB, which covers the $185/month Medicare Part B premium but not deductibles or copays. It's still $2,220/year in savings.

faqs.question: Does she need to reapply every year?
faqs.answer: No, but the county will review her eligibility annually. Income and asset limits may change yearly based on federal poverty guidelines.

faqs.question: What happens if QI funding runs out?
faqs.answer: She'll be placed on a waiting list for the next year's funding. SLMB and QMB don't have funding caps, so check if her income qualifies for those programs.

faqs.question: Can she have this and Medicare Advantage?
faqs.answer: Yes. These programs work with both Original Medicare and Medicare Advantage plans to cover premiums and cost-sharing.

faqs.question: Will this affect her Social Security benefits?
faqs.answer: No. These Medicare Savings Programs don't impact Social Security, Medicare benefits, or other federal programs.

programs.name: Home and Community-Based Services (HCBS) Waivers
programs.tagline: Get home care services for your parent instead of nursing home placement — from daily care help to meal delivery.

localEntities.name: 47 contracted Case Management Agencies (regional); county human/social services offices

programs.intro:
If your parent needs daily help with bathing, dressing, or medication but wants to stay home instead of entering a nursing home, HCBS Waivers can provide those services at home. These waivers cover personal care, homemaker services, home-delivered meals, home modifications, emergency response systems, and other support services tailored to your parent's specific needs.

Colorado has 10 different HCBS waivers targeting specific populations. The Elderly, Blind, and Disabled (EBD) waiver serves people 65+ or those 18-64 with qualifying disabilities. Your parent must meet nursing facility level of care (meaning they need daily help with activities like bathing, dressing, or medication management) and have very low income — typically 300% or more below the Federal Poverty Level.

These are not guaranteed benefits. Colorado has limited slots and waitlists are common. You'll need to apply through both your county office (for financial eligibility) and a regional Case Management Agency (for functional assessment).

summary: Very low income (300%+ below poverty level)
At risk of nursing home placement
structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must need nursing facility level of care — meaning your parent requires daily assistance with activities like bathing, dressing, eating, or medication management
otherRequirements: At risk of nursing home placement
Live in home or plan to return home

applicationGuide.summary: Apply through both your county office (financial review) and regional Case Management Agency (care assessment) — no single application timeline specified.

steps.title: Contact your county human services office
steps.description: Start with financial eligibility screening. Bring income and asset documentation.

steps.title: Get functional assessment from Case Management Agency
steps.description: A separate agency will evaluate whether your parent meets nursing facility level of care using the ULTC 100.2 assessment.

steps.title: Choose the right waiver program
steps.description: Colorado has 10 different HCBS waivers. Make sure you're applying for the right one based on your parent's age and condition.

steps.title: Join waitlist if no immediate slots
steps.description: Limited slots available. You can be on waitlist for one waiver while receiving services from another.

applicationGuide.waitlist: Yes, limited slots available. Waitlists are common due to funding constraints.
applicationGuide.tip: You can only receive one HCBS waiver at a time, but you can be on the waitlist for multiple programs.

contentSections.heading: Asset Limits: What Counts
excluded: Home where spouse or dependent relative lives

categories.name: Medical
items: Medical records showing need for daily care

contentSections.text: HCBS Waivers have limited slots and waitlists are common. This is not a guaranteed benefit even if your parent qualifies.
Family members and friends can be paid as caregivers under some HCBS waivers like In-Home Support Services. Ask about this option during assessment.

faqs.question: Can Mom keep her house if she qualifies for HCBS?
faqs.answer: Yes, she can keep her home as long as the equity doesn't exceed $1,130,000 in 2026. The home is exempt from asset limits, and staying home is actually the goal of these waivers.

faqs.question: What if she needs more care than the waiver provides?
faqs.answer: Services are individualized based on assessment, but if her needs exceed what can be safely provided at home, she may need to consider nursing home care or look into other waiver programs.

faqs.question: Can I get paid to take care of my parent through this program?
faqs.answer: Yes, under some HCBS waivers like In-Home Support Services, family members can be hired and paid as caregivers. Discuss this option during your case management assessment.

faqs.question: How long is the waitlist?
faqs.answer: Waitlist times aren't specified and vary by region and waiver type. You can be on a waitlist for one waiver while receiving services from another if you qualify for multiple programs.

faqs.question: What's the difference between the 10 different waivers?
faqs.answer: Each targets specific populations. The Elderly, Blind, and Disabled (EBD) waiver serves people 65+ or disabled adults 18-64. Others focus on mental health, developmental disabilities, or other conditions. You can only receive one at a time.

faqs.question: What happens if her income is just over the limit?
faqs.answer: Income must be 300% or more below Federal Poverty Level. Contact your county office for exact current limits — these change annually and vary by household size. If over the limit, she won't qualify for this program.

programs.name: Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly (PACE)
programs.tagline: If your parent needs nursing home-level care but wants to stay home, PACE coordinates all their medical care under one team.
programs.intro:
If your parent is 55+ and needs nursing home-level care, PACE lets them stay in their community while receiving comprehensive care coordination. Because PACE covers all medical services under one team, your parent gets primary care, nursing, therapy, transportation, and home care from coordinated providers — no juggling separate doctors or services.

PACE is only available in specific Colorado counties and zip codes. Your parent must be Medicare-eligible and clinically assessed as needing nursing home-level care (daily help with bathing, dressing, medication management, or mobility). If they qualify for Medicaid, it covers most costs. If not, they pay the Medicaid portion out-of-pocket while Medicare covers its share.

The interdisciplinary team includes a primary care provider, registered nurse, social worker, physical/occupational/recreational therapists, dietitian, home care coordinator, and transportation driver who create a customized care plan for your parent's specific needs.

structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must meet Colorado's nursing home level of care (needs daily help with activities like bathing, dressing, medication management, or mobility as certified by PACE assessment team)
otherRequirements: Lives in designated PACE service area
Able to live safely in community with PACE support

applicationGuide.summary: Call for a home visit assessment; state approval takes 7-14 days after team evaluation.

steps.title: Call Colorado PACE
steps.description: Call 888-788-1241 to speak with an Enrollment Specialist who will schedule a home visit and explain services available in your area.

steps.title: Home assessment
steps.description: The interdisciplinary team visits your parent at home to assess medical, social, and functional needs including cognitive and mobility evaluation.

steps.title: Team review
steps.description: The team determines if your parent meets nursing home level of care requirements and can safely live in the community with PACE support.

steps.title: State approval
steps.description: Colorado Department of Health Care Policy and Financing reviews the assessment and makes final eligibility determination within 7-14 days.

applicationGuide.tip: PACE staff will help determine Medicaid eligibility during the process, which affects how much your parent pays.

contentSections.text: PACE is only available in specific Colorado counties and zip codes. Your parent must agree to receive ALL medical care through the PACE team — they cannot keep their current doctor outside the program.
If your parent doesn't qualify for Medicaid now, PACE staff provide Medicaid planning to help them meet asset criteria and reduce out-of-pocket costs.

faqs.question: Can Mom keep her house if she qualifies for PACE?
faqs.answer: Yes. The primary home doesn't count toward the $2,000 asset limit for Medicaid eligibility, and PACE is designed to help people stay in their homes and communities rather than move to nursing facilities.

faqs.question: What if Dad's income is too high for Medicaid but he needs PACE services?
faqs.answer: He can still enroll in PACE. Medicare will cover its portion of costs, and he'll pay the Medicaid portion out-of-pocket. PACE staff can help with Medicaid planning strategies to potentially qualify for coverage later.

faqs.question: Does PACE cover transportation to medical appointments?
faqs.answer: Yes. PACE includes transportation services as part of the care team. A transportation driver coordinates rides to medical appointments, the PACE center, and other covered services in your parent's care plan.

faqs.question: What happens if Mom needs to move to a nursing home later?
faqs.answer: PACE covers nursing home care if your parent's needs change. Because the same interdisciplinary team manages all levels of care, they coordinate the transition while maintaining continuity of medical and social services.

faqs.question: Can we switch back to Mom's regular doctors if PACE doesn't work out?
faqs.answer: Yes, but there are enrollment periods and rules. Once enrolled, your parent receives all care through PACE providers. You can disenroll during specific periods, but discuss timing with PACE staff to avoid gaps in coverage.

faqs.question: How do we know if our area has PACE services?
faqs.answer: Call 888-788-1241 with your parent's zip code. Colorado PACE serves specific counties and areas — the enrollment specialist will immediately tell you if services are available in your location.

programs.name: Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program (SNAP)
programs.tagline: Up to $298/month on an EBT card for groceries if your parent is on a fixed income.

localEntities.name: County Department of Human Services (statewide via PEAK)

programs.intro:
If your parent is 60+ and struggling to afford groceries on Social Security or a pension, SNAP provides monthly benefits loaded onto an EBT card for food purchases. Your parent could receive $100–$298 per month depending on their income and household size.

Because Colorado has no asset limits for most households, your parent can keep their home, car, and retirement savings and still qualify. The key test is monthly income: $2,608 or less for a single person, $3,526 for a couple (2025-2026 limits).

SNAP benefits work like a debit card at grocery stores, farmers markets, and many online retailers. You cannot use SNAP for alcohol, tobacco, or hot prepared foods, but it covers most groceries including meat, dairy, produce, and pantry staples.
programs.savingsSource: Monthly benefit amounts of $100-298 for individuals based on income

otherRequirements: U.S. citizen or lawful permanent resident 5+ years

applicationGuide.summary: Apply online through Colorado PEAK — processing takes up to several weeks.

steps.title: Create PEAK account
steps.description: Go to cdhs.colorado.gov/PEAK and create an online account with your parent's information.

steps.title: Complete SNAP application
steps.description: Fill out the online form with income, expenses, and household details. The system will calculate eligibility automatically.

steps.title: Upload documents
steps.description: Scan and upload proof of income (Social Security statements), identity (driver's license), and medical expenses over $35/month if applicable.

steps.title: Schedule interview
steps.description: Your county office will contact you to schedule a brief phone or in-person interview to verify information.

steps.title: Receive EBT card
steps.description: If approved, your parent will receive an EBT card in the mail within 7-10 days with benefits loaded monthly.

applicationGuide.tip: If your parent has medical expenses over $35/month (Medicare premiums, prescriptions, doctor visits), gather those receipts — they can increase benefits significantly.

contentSections.heading: Income Limits by Household Size (2025-2026)
contentSections.footnote: Limits effective October 2025 - September 2026. If over these amounts, you may still qualify through net income calculation.

contentSections.heading: Asset Limits: What Counts (Only if income is over limits above)
included: Second vehicles beyond one per household

categories.name: Identity & Residency
items: Utility bill or lease showing Colorado address

contentSections.text: If your parent's income is slightly over the limit, don't give up. Medical expenses over $35/month (including Medicare premiums) are deducted from income, which can bring them under the threshold and increase benefit amounts.

faqs.question: Can my parent keep their house and car if they get SNAP?
faqs.answer: Yes. Colorado doesn't count your parent's home (any value) or one vehicle toward asset limits. Most households have no asset limits at all. Only if income is over $2,608/month (single person) do asset limits apply, and even then the limit is $4,500 with major exemptions.

faqs.question: What if my parent's Social Security is just over the income limit?
faqs.answer: They may still qualify through net income calculation. Medical expenses over $35/month (Medicare premiums, prescriptions, doctor visits) are deducted from income. High utility costs also get deducted. Many seniors qualify this way even with Social Security over the initial limits.

faqs.question: How much will my parent actually receive in SNAP benefits?
faqs.answer: It depends on net income after deductions. Maximum is $298/month for one person, but most receive less. Every $100 in net income reduces benefits by about $30. Someone with $1,500 Social Security after medical deductions might receive $150-200/month.

faqs.question: Can my parent use SNAP benefits for online grocery delivery?
faqs.answer: Yes. Most major retailers (Amazon, Walmart, Safeway) accept EBT online in Colorado. Your parent can shop online and pay with their EBT card. Delivery fees must be paid separately with cash or credit card.

faqs.question: What happens if my parent misses the annual renewal?
faqs.answer: Benefits stop immediately. Colorado requires renewal every 6-12 months plus annual change reports. Mark renewal dates on your calendar and respond quickly to any county letters. If benefits lapse, your parent must reapply and wait for processing again.

faqs.question: Does getting SNAP affect other benefits like Medicare or Social Security?
faqs.answer: No. SNAP doesn't count as income for other programs and won't reduce Social Security or Medicare benefits. In fact, SNAP qualification can help your parent access other programs like reduced Medicare premiums through Medicare Savings Programs.
